data.title: Trump's new tariffs hit with lawsuit
data.description:
President Trump’s new tariffs were hit a legal challenge Friday from two small businesses who are represented by part of the legal team that toppled Trump’s earlier tariffs at the Supreme Court. 

The new surcharge, between 10 percent and 12.5 percent on goods imported from 60 trading partners, is the administration’s latest attempt to unilaterally impose the president’s tariff agenda under existing laws. 

Just like the previous statutes Trump invoked, the lawsuit argues the administration is now stretching Section 301 of the Trade Act of 1974 too far. 

“It is not a freestanding authorization to tax substantially all imports from substantially all trading partners,” the lawsuit reads. 

The Hill has reached out to the White House for comment.

The administration announced the Section 301 tariffs on Thursday, just as the president’s previous ones were expiring, which were put in place under a temporary authority.

Tariffs under Section 301 can last longer, but they must be in response to unfair trade practices that are found to restrict the nation’s commerce. The administration conducted a monthslong investigation into forced labor practices overseas, which included extensive public comments and two rounds of hearings.  

The lawsuit claims that’s a pretext. It also argues that U.S. Trade Representative (USTR) Jamieson Greer’s announcement needed to take a “country-specific” approach.

“Section 301 does not permit USTR to substitute a generalized assertion that forced labor is harmful, unfair, or economically distortive worldwide for the statute’s required determination concerning an identified foreign act, policy, or practice and its burden or restriction on United States commerce,” the complaint states. 

The lawsuit is brought by spice and e-commerce business Burlap & Barrel and high-end watch retailer Collective Horology.

They are represented by the Liberty Justice Center. The libertarian public-interest firm has long been challenging Trump’s tariff agenda in court. It was part of the team that defeated his earlier, emergency tariffs at the Supreme Court earlier this year.

data.title: Education Department removes anti-discrimination rule
data.description:
The Education Department said Thursday that it is rescinding a long-standing policy allowing statistical disparities to serve as evidence of discrimination in schools during civil rights investigations.

The policy, which took place immediately, eliminated guidance dating back to 1979 that permitted the agency’s Office for Civil Rights to use data showing disproportionate outcomes for students of different races when investigating alleged violations of Title VI of the Civil Rights Act.

Federal investigators now can no longer rely on statistical disparities alone to support discrimination findings and instead will have to prove intentional discrimination in civil rights complaints against schools using other means.

“A child is so much more than a number in a data set. The left’s notion of ‘equity’ should never prevent teachers from keeping their classrooms and students safe,” Assistant Secretary for Civil Rights Kimberly Richey said in a statement. “With these changes, schools will be empowered to address classroom issues without fearing the federal government will weaponize antidiscrimination laws.”

Richey added that the changes will “help ensure fair enforcement of Title VI, as intended by Congress,” and that it would give schools greater flexibility to address disciplinary issues without fear of federal investigations.

“The Trump Administration will always hold schools accountable for discrimination and ensure all students in America receive the equal educational opportunities and equal treatment to which they are entitled by law,” she said.

The department argued previous administrations used disparate impact analysis to pressure schools into considering race when making disciplinary decisions, claiming that if data showed minority students were disciplined at higher rates than white students, the schools could face civil rights investigations.

In a joint statement, 60 civil rights and education organizations condemned the change, saying it “has no basis in law or morality” and accused the administration of abandoning longstanding civil rights protections.

“For decades, courts have acknowledged that discrimination is not always explicit or overt. The disparate impact tool is designed to remove unjustified barriers in education and ensure that all students have an equal opportunity to learn and succeed,” the statement reads.

The groups added that rescinding the policy “will clear the way for discrimination that too often deprives students of equal educational opportunity based on race, color, and national origin.”

data.title: NYC police investigating two Upper West Side stabbings as possible hate crimes
data.description:
The New York City Police Department (NYPD) is investigating two stabbings as possible hate crimes, police said Thursday. 

An Asian man and a Jewish man were injured in separate attacks, which occurred midday Thursday in the Upper West Side. Both men were transported to Mount Sinai-St. Luke’s Hospital to receive treatment for their injuries and are expected to recover, according to NYPD Commissioner Jessica Tisch.  

She said in a statement shared on social media Thursday that 51-year-old Raul Morales has been arrested for suspected connection to these stabbings and that Morales allegedly yelled “Allahu Akbar” during both attacks. 

A spokesperson with the NYPD’s public information office said that police responded to these attacks around 1:30 p.m. and that the victims were found “conscious and alert.” A 57-year-old man sustained a stab wound to his back, and a 50-year-old man was found with a wound on his chest, according to the spokesperson.

Tisch said the police department is investigating whether this was a potential hate crime or if mental health issues played a role in these attacks. The commissioner said there is no known connection between Morales and either of the victims. 

“I want to commend the bravery of a good Samaritan who led our officers to the location where the perpetrator was hiding, as well as our NYPD cops who brought an end to the incident without further injuries,” Tisch wrote. 

New York City Mayor Zohran Mamdani (D) condemned the attacks, in a statement shared on social media, and commended the NYPD’s response to the incident. 

“These hateful and despicable attacks have no place in our city,” the mayor said. 

Eric Goldstein, the CEO of UJA-Federation of New York, said he was “horrified and heartbroken” by the attacks but “no longer surprised” at a press conference on Friday morning. 

He called on Mamdani to turn down the temperature on his criticism of Israel and its leadership, saying the mayor “contributes to a climate in which Jewish New Yorkers are increasingly targeted and unsafe.”

Mamdani has been critical of Israel’s military actions in Gaza and recently reupped his call for the arrest of Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu ahead of the foreign leader’s visit to New York City for the United Nations General Assembly this fall.

“Mayor Mamdani, stop fanning the flames of hatred and division,” Goldstein said. “The consequences are not abstract. They’re playing out on our streets.”

New York Gov. Kathy Hochul (D) also spoke out against these acts of violence. 

“Disgusted by what appears to be another senseless attack on New Yorkers simply because they’re Jewish,” she wrote in a Thursday social media post.

“The strength of New York has always been our ability to live together across every faith and background,” the governor continued. “Anyone who tries to tear that apart will be met with the full force of the law. New York will stand with our Jewish community today, tomorrow, and always.”

data.title: Live updates: Trump's new tariffs set to take effect; DNC panel picks SC to kick off 2028 primaries
data.description:
A panel of the Democratic National Committee (DNC) on Friday chose South Carolina to lead the 2028 primary calendar. Nevada came in second, and New Hampshire, which had long served as the first-in-the-nation primary state, came in third, when it came down to a vote. 

President Trump’s 10 percent tariffs are set to expire Friday, but U.S. Trade Representative Jamieson Greer announced new levies Thursday to replace them.

The fresh import taxes — ranging from 10 percent to 12.5 percent — will hit 60 countries, accounting for 99 percent of U.S. imports. They are pegged to the elimination of forced labor. International trading partners ripped the new tariffs Friday.

U.S. forces have launched strikes against Iran for nearly two weeks straight, and Republican defense hawks are urging Trump to unleash major combat operations. 

Trump will speak Friday evening at the rescheduled White House Correspondents’ Association dinner. The association said earlier this week it will honor a Secret Service agent who was pivotal in stopping a gunman who tried to assassinate Trump during the annual dinner this spring.

The House is now on a five-week recess, while the Senate is set to return Monday. And tension is rising between Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) and the White House as the upper chamber struggles to pass Trump’s key priority: the SAVE America Act.

Follow live below.

data.title: Trump presses Thune to move reconciliation framework before August recess as tensions mount
data.description:
President Trump on Friday pressured Senate Majority Leader John Thune (R-S.D.) to immediately take up a House-passed budget resolution to advance critical funding for the Iran war despite Thune’s statement on Thursday that the measure doesn’t have 50 votes to pass the Senate.

Trump turned the screws on the Senate GOP leader to act on the House-passed bill two days after complaining at a rally in Georgia that “the Senate is a place you send things when you want them to die.”

“I am asking that the Senate adopt a Budget Resolution, which can pass with a simple Majority, before leaving for the August recess. We are ready to work side-by-side with the Senate to get this done for the American People!” Trump posted on Truth Social Friday afternoon.

The call to action came a day after Thune told reporters that he doesn’t have 50 votes to advance the $95 billion House-passed budget bill, which includes $73 billion in funding for the Pentagon and intelligence agencies, $12 billion for farmers and $10 billion for election-related reforms.

“As I’ve said before, you got to get to 50, and I can’t count to 50 right now on a budget resolution,” Thune said when asked about how the Senate would handle the House-passed budget framework.

“We’ll see. We’ll have those conversations with the conference. The priority is funding the government. To me, that’s the thing we got to keep our eyes on,” he said.

The latest pressure from Trump comes amid escalating tensions between the White House and Thune.

White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said on Thursday that Trump’s “patience is running out” with Thune and “the Senate needs to do its job.”

That criticism prompted a testy response from Thune.

“Maybe she or somebody else ought to get on the phone and get the votes. Right? It’s 50 around here,” Thune shot back.

“Instead of pointing the finger at Republicans, they might think about going after the people who are stopping it on the floor, which is the Democrats,” Thune added. “And if there are Republicans they think are gettable, get on the phone.”

Thune told reporters on Tuesday that he plans to “hold” the House-passed budget framework in the Senate to use as a potential vehicle to advance a government funding stopgap in September.

Passage of a joint budget resolution through both chambers unlocks the budget reconciliation process, which would allow Republicans to pass legislation with a simple-majority vote and circumvent a Democratic filibuster.

But previous efforts to pass the SAVE America Act, which would require people to show proof of citizenship when registering to vote and require photo identification to vote, through the Senate have failed. Senate parliamentarian Elizabeth MacDonough has ruled that Trump’s No. 1 legislative priority is not eligible to pass with a simple majority under the rules that govern the budget reconciliation process.

Republican senators are also split over whether to offset the cost of the $95 billion package with other spending cuts.

data.title: Cyclospora outbreak linked to lettuce expands to 9 states
data.description:
Federal health officials have linked cyclospora outbreaks in four additional states to the same iceberg lettuce sourced from central Mexico that has sickened thousands of people in the Midwest. 

The new states are Illinois, Kansas, Oklahoma and Pennsylvania. They join Michigan, Ohio, Indiana, Kentucky and West Virginia as part of the country’s largest known cyclosporiasis outbreak, according to an update posted Friday. 

The latest news does mean that cases are spreading or accelerating, but indicates investigators are closing in on a common link between outbreaks. 

The Food and Drug Administration (FDA) said the infections are likely tied to iceberg lettuce supplied by Taylor Farms and grown in central Mexico. Though there was no positive test of produce to confirm the link, epidemiological evidence including interviews with people who got sick gave strong signals. 

“FDA’s traceback investigation and outbreak data continue to converge on shredded iceberg lettuce from Taylor Farms from growers in central Mexico,” the agency said. “We encourage the public to continue to avoid all products listed in Taylor Farms’ voluntary recall.”

According to the health agency, a total of 1,947 people infected with cyclospora and reporting exposure to Taco Bell have been reported by nine states. Illnesses started on dates ranging from June 22 to July 20. There have been at least 98 hospitalizations, and no deaths. 

Taylor Farms issued a voluntary recall for all iceberg lettuce sourced from its central Mexican facility and Taco Bell pulled the affected lettuce from its restaurants. 

The FDA said it expects to continue to see the number of confirmed cases rise, despite the recall, because it can take as long as six weeks to determine if a sick person is part of the outbreak.  

The federal count lags behind state numbers and only includes confirmed cases, so some of the states hit by the outbreak have reported much higher numbers of infections. 

Nationwide, the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) has confirmed or is investigating more than 11,500 cases of cyclosporiasis. But not every case is connected. 

On Thursday, the FDA said it was looking a new outbreak with 72 cases but did not identify the location or the potential source. 

Cyclosporiasis is a foodborne infection caused by a parasite that’s typically spread through food or water contaminated with human feces. Not everyone who is exposed will display symptoms, but the main one is frequent and watery diarrhea.

data.title: Ex-federal prosecutor on Jack Smith developments: 'This is preposterous'
data.description:
Ex-federal prosecutor Andrew Weissmann called House Judiciary Committee Chair Jim Jordan’s (R-Ohio) recommendation of criminal charges for former special counsel Jack Smith “preposterous.”

In an interview on MSNOW with Jen Psaki, Weissmann added, “You cannot read this letter and in any way view this as perjury.”

He is referring to Jordan’s accusation that Smith made false statements to Congress when he was deposed late last year about his investigation and prosecution of Trump. During the hearing, Smith was asked whether he had subpoenaed the text messages of members of Congress which he denied.

Smith did review the texts of lawmakers but received the messages through the White House. Jordan did not think Smith was candid enough and is recommending the prosecution of the man who led the criminal cases against President Trump.

“He was asked, did you get the content of text messages from members of Congress through a search warrant? Did you get the content of text messages of members of Congress through a search warrant? To which he said, no, I didn’t,” Weissmann recalled.

Rep. Jamie Raskin (Md.), the top Democrat on the panel, confirmed Smith did not subpoena members’ text messages but rather compelled the National Archives to turn over the communications of White House staff.

“They did not even ask whether Mr. Smith’s team had ever reviewed text messages from Members. Jack Smith could not have lied because Republican Members never even asked the question their theory is based on,” Raskin wrote in his statement.

“That is 100% truthful,” Weissmann agreed. “The fact that Jack Smith also got documents from the national archives was not asked in any way, shape, or form.”

“I usually am more careful to say, you know, it seems like or we don’t know everything they have. You cannot read this letter and in any way view this as perjury,” Weissmann continued. 

Under Trump, the Department of Justice (DOJ) has pursued other prosecutions against political opponents, including New York Attorney General Letitia James (D) and former FBI Director James Comey.

The DOJ is also reportedly looking into a case for perjury against E. Jean Carroll, a writer who accused President Trump of sexual assault.

Smith in an interview on MSNOW’s “Deadline: White House” has said it “angers” him to see public servants who work on cases that aren’t favorable to Trump or his allies “demonized for doing their jobs” by the Trump administration.

data.title: US exceeds measles cases in all of 2025 by July 2026
data.description:
The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention’s (CDC) tally of 2026 measles cases has officially surpassed the annual total of last year, with more then 2,300 cases reported from across most of the country.

As of July 23, the CDC has confirmed 2,318 new measles cases in 2026, compared with 2,289 total cases detected in 2025. Johns Hopkins University’s measles tracker had noted the milestone earlier this week, being updated sooner than the CDC.

According to the agency, the majority of the cases from this year have come from 45 U.S. jurisdictions as 35 new outbreaks have been reported.

This tally marks the worst year for measles the U.S. has seen in roughly 35 years. Measles cases in the U.S. plummeted in 1990 following the rollout of an aggressive vaccination campaign and the introduction of the two-dose measles vaccine schedule.

The U.S. eliminated the disease in 2000, but that distinction is in question. A meeting with the Pan American Health Organization will take place in November to review the U.S.’s and Mexico’s respective elimination statuses. Canada lost its elimination status last year.

Measles is highly infectious, with the potential to infect 9 out of 10 unprotected people exposed to someone with the disease. A 95 percent vaccination rate is considered ideal for preventing outbreaks. Among U.S. kindergarteners, this vaccine rate has fallen to about 92 percent.

While this represents only a few percentage points, infectious disease experts have warned that the spate of outbreaks in the past few years show how easily an outbreak can occur in pockets where immunization rates are particularly low.

“As measles cases continue to surge across the U.S., now is the time to make sure you and your family are protected. Getting vaccinated remains our strongest defense against this disease that is both highly contagious and entirely preventable,” Dr. Toluwalasè A. Ajayi, board chair of the American Medical Association, said in a statement Friday.

“The MMR vaccine has protected our communities for decades. There is no cure for measles, but vaccination is a safe and effective way to prevent it,” Ajayi added. “Choosing to vaccinate protects not only you and your family, but also babies, people with weakened immune systems, and others who can’t be vaccinated. Protecting yourself helps protect your entire community. If you have questions about measles, please talk with your doctor.”

The Democratic National Committee (DNC) blasted the Trump administration for the continued measles transmission.

“As communities across the country face outbreaks of preventable diseases, RFK Jr. and [President] Trump continue to sow doubt and dysfunction into the public health system and gut critical agencies, putting families and communities in harm’s way,” DNC spokesperson Jaelin O’Halloran said in a statement, referring to Health and Human Services Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr.

While a longtime skeptic of vaccinations, Kennedy did endorse the measles vaccine last year when outbreaks began to spread across the country, saying, “The most effective way to prevent the spread of measles is the MMR vaccine.”

data.title: Elon Musk says he backs 'normal people'  when asked about support for 'far right'
data.description:
Tesla and SpaceX CEO Elon Musk is pushing back on the idea that he supports the “far right,” quipping in a recent interview that he backs “normal people” and slamming the media for its coverage of conservatives.

In an interview published by The Economist Thursday, editor-in-chief Zanny Minton Beddoes suggested Musk “supports not just the populist right, but the far right — in fact, very fringe parties in some countries.”

Musk, a former ally and adviser to President Trump, interjected, saying, “No, I support the normal people. What you call the far right, falsely.”

“Literally, you could go back 10 or 15 years and these policies were completely normal,” the tech mogul continued.

Beddoes said she agrees that “the center of gravity in this country has shifted, certainly the Democratic Party shifted to the left, but that’s not what I’m talking about.”

She said she was discussing figures such as Rupert Lowe, a member of the United Kingdom Parliament who leads the far right political party Restore Britain.

“It’s just normal people,” Musk repeated. “Here are the principles, and tell me, which of these sound terrible. That we should have secure borders? That we should have safe cities? That we should have sensible spending? Which of those three are far right fringe?”

When Beddoes tried to answer, Musk said he wanted to “admonish” her and the media for “the absurd characterization of the far right.”

“It is false and misleading and nonsense,” he added.

Musk has a history of weighing in on international and domestic politics, often slamming the left for its politics and the mainstream media for its coverage choices.

At the start of 2025, he backed the far right German political party Alternative for Germany (AfD), while urging the release of far right activist Tommy Robinson.

While joining Trump on the campaign trail and later serving as the head of the so-called Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE), Musk frequently expressed support for Republican politics and poured in more than $250 million to back Trump’s return to the White House.

During his time at DOGE, he and his companies faced intense backlash over the initiative’s sweeping job and funding cuts across the federal government.

Later in the interview, Musk suggested “he got carried away” during his brief time in the political world.

“I think I got a little too involved in politics,” Musk said. “I got carried away, frankly.”

Since Musk’s departure from DOGE in late May 2025, the initiatives waned down, and DOGE officially shut down operations on July 4.

data.title: Democratic panel picks South Carolina as first primary state
data.description:
South Carolina is expected to be the first state in the 2028 Democratic presidential primary contest after the Democratic National Committee’s Rules and Bylaws committee voted Friday to approve it as the initial battleground.

The committee’s recommendation will now go to the entire Democratic National Committee, which will consider the matter in Austin, Texas next month. 

The committee vote follows a months-long process where more than a dozen states made the case for appearing first on the primary calendar. 

In the final days before committee members cast their votes, surrogates for Nevada made a last minute appeal to committee members. But South Carolina — with its loyal and influential electorate of Black voters — ultimately won out with 26 votes. 

Nevada came in 2nd with 19 votes, and New Hampshire, which had long served as the first-in-the-nation primary state, came in third with 4 votes. 

The DNC committee also voted on Friday to expand the early contest window in 2028 from five states to six states. 

“I’ve said from the beginning that I care more about the early window as a whole than who goes first of the first set of states,” said Michael Kapp, one of the members who voted on Friday. “With the addition of a sixth state, our eventual nominee will have to run through a gauntlet before ever reaching Super Tuesday. 

“Every region, every constituency of this party will have a voice in the early window and be able to shape the race,” Kapp added. “Ensuring we win in November 2028 is the priority — this calendar will get us there.”
